name: Central African Republic
tagline: From the megaliths of Bouar to a fragile peace at the heart of Africa
summary: Landlocked at the continent's centre, the Central African Republic carries one of Africa's deepest archaeological records in the standing stones of Bouar and one of its harshest modern histories. Slave-raiding from the Sudanic states and traders such as al-Zubayr depopulated much of the Ubangi region in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries; French conquest then bound it into a brutal concessionary-company regime within French Equatorial Africa. Independent since 13 August 1960, the country endured the imperial pretensions of Jean-Bédel Bokassa, repeated coups, and a sectarian civil war after the 2013 Séléka seizure of power, leaving it dependent on the UN mission MINUSCA and foreign actors for a precarious stability.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: The territory of Ubangi-Shari became the fully independent Central African Republic on 13 August 1960, with David Dacko — a relative and successor of the nationalist founder Barthélemy Boganda — as its first president.

eras.title: Megaliths and Early Peoples
eras.standfirst: Standing stones near Bouar and centuries of migration set the deepest record of the Ubangi watershed.
body: The oldest monuments of the Central African Republic are the megaliths around Bouar in the west, where farmers raised standing stones weighing several tons. Conventionally placed in the late Neolithic, roughly between 3500 and 2700 BCE, the alignments — known locally as tazunu — imply organised labour and durable communities long before any written record of the region. They remain among the most significant prehistoric sites in Central Africa and sit on the country's tentative list for UNESCO World Heritage recognition.

Over the following millennia the region became a meeting ground of language families. Ubangian-speaking peoples settled along the Ubangi River, Central Sudanic groups moved in from the north and east, and Bantu-speakers spread from the south-west; ironworking is attested in the region from around the first millennium BCE. These overlapping migrations produced the dense ethnic mosaic — Gbaya, Banda, Ngbaka, Sara, Mandjia, Zande and Nzakara among others — that still defines the country.

Because the savannas of the centre lay between the forest and the Sahel, they were a corridor for trade in copper, salt, dried fish and textiles rather than the seat of a single great empire. That openness later left the scattered populations of the watershed acutely exposed to the mounted slave-raiders who would descend from the Sudanic kingdoms to the north.

events.title: The megaliths of Bouar are raised
events.summary: Farmers near Bouar in the west erected multi-ton standing stones (tazunu), among the most important prehistoric monuments in Central Africa and evidence of organised Neolithic communities.

events.title: Ironworking and migration
events.summary: Ubangian, Central Sudanic and Bantu-speaking peoples settled the watershed and ironworking spread, laying down the ethnic patchwork of Gbaya, Banda, Zande, Nzakara and others.

eras.title: Zande States and the Slave Trade
eras.standfirst: Zande and Bandia kingdoms rose along the Ubangi as slave-raiding from the Sudanic north hollowed out the savanna.
body: From the eighteenth century the Zande (Azande) people coalesced under the Avongara aristocracy and expanded across the south-east of the modern country into what is now South Sudan and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. Alongside them the Ngbandi-speaking Bandia clan, who adopted Zande and Nzakara language and custom, built kingdoms along the Ubangi River — most notably the Nzakara-Bandia state and the sultanate of Bangassou, consolidated around 1876–1890 under Sultan Bangassou, together with neighbouring Rafai and Zemio.

These states were caught between two predatory frontiers. From the north, the Sudanic sultanates of Wadai and Bagirmi, and their tributary Dar al-Kuti, mounted slaving expeditions (razzias) deep into the Banda and Nzakara country. The most devastating pressure came from the Sudanese warlord al-Zubayr (Rabih az-Zubayr) and his successors: from the 1870s their armies raided the upper Ubangi for captives, and in 1890 Rabih installed Muhammad al-Sanusi as ruler of Dar al-Kuti, who turned slave-raiding into the organising business of his fortified base at Ndélé.

The cumulative effect was catastrophic depopulation across the centre and east of the region. Whole districts were emptied of people carried north into slavery, and the survivors were left scattered and impoverished — a demographic wound that helped leave the territory, when Europeans arrived, both fragmented and sparsely settled.

events.title: Zande and Bandia kingdoms expand
events.summary: Under the Avongara nobility the Zande spread across the south-east, while the Bandia clan founded Ubangi River states including the Nzakara kingdom and the sultanate of Bangassou.

events.title: Dar al-Kuti founded under Wadai
events.summary: The sultanate of Dar al-Kuti was established as a tributary of Dar Runga and ultimately of Wadai, becoming a staging ground for slave raids into the Banda and Nzakara lands to the south.

events.title: Al-Zubayr installs al-Sanusi at Dar al-Kuti
events.summary: The Sudanese warlord Rabih az-Zubayr (al-Zubayr) deposed Kobur and installed Muhammad al-Sanusi, who from his base at Ndélé intensified the razzias that depopulated much of the Ubangi region.

pullquote.text: Raiding parties from the Sudanic north carried away captives by the thousands, hollowing out the savanna long before the French arrived.

eras.title: Conquest and the Concession Regime
eras.standfirst: France seized the Ubangi watershed and leased it to concessionary companies whose rubber and ivory exactions cost countless lives.
body: France advanced into the region from the Congo in the late 1880s, founding a post at Bangui in 1889 and pressing inland against the slave-raiding sultanates and Zande states. The territory was organised as the colony of Ubangi-Shari (Oubangui-Chari), and resistance was broken piecemeal: Bangassou and Rafai were brought under protectorate in the 1890s, and Muhammad al-Sanusi of Dar al-Kuti was killed by French forces in 1911, with his successor crushed the following year.

From 1899 the French government leased vast tracts of Ubangi-Shari to private concessionary companies — among them the Company of the Upper Ubangi Sultanates, granted some 140,000 square kilometres — giving them near-total power over the inhabitants. On the model of the neighbouring Congo Free State, company militias extracted wild rubber and ivory through forced labour, hostage-taking and violence. The French writer André Gide, travelling through the region, exposed some of these abuses in his Voyage au Congo (1927).

The forced-labour system reached its most lethal in conscription for the Congo–Ocean railway in the neighbouring colony, where thousands of workers recruited across French Equatorial Africa, including from Ubangi-Shari, died of disease, accident and exhaustion. The combined weight of taxation, rubber quotas and labour levies fell on a population already thinned by the slave trade.

events.title: France founds Bangui
events.summary: French forces established a post at Bangui on the Ubangi River, the base from which they extended control over the territory that became the colony of Ubangi-Shari.

events.title: Concessionary companies take over
events.summary: France leased huge tracts of Ubangi-Shari to private companies such as the Company of the Upper Ubangi Sultanates, whose militias exacted rubber and ivory through brutal forced labour.

events.title: Death of al-Sanusi of Dar al-Kuti
events.summary: French forces killed Muhammad al-Sanusi at Ndélé, ending the slave-raiding sultanate of Dar al-Kuti; his successor's resistance was suppressed by 1912.

eras.title: French Equatorial Africa to Independence
eras.standfirst: A colony within French Equatorial Africa, scarred by the Kongo-Wara revolt, found its national voice in Barthélemy Boganda.
body: In 1910 Ubangi-Shari was incorporated into the federation of French Equatorial Africa, alongside Gabon, Middle Congo and Chad, governed from Brazzaville. Cotton cultivation was imposed alongside continued labour levies, and grievances boiled over in the Kongo-Wara rebellion of 1928–1931 — the 'War of the Hoe Handle' — when the Gbaya prophet Karinou (Karnou) drew hundreds of thousands of followers against forced labour and recruitment for the Congo–Ocean railway. The French killed Karinou in December 1928 and suppressed the revolt over the following three years at a cost of many thousands of lives.

After the Second World War colonial subjects gained limited political representation, and in 1946 a Roman Catholic priest, Barthélemy Boganda, was elected as Ubangi-Shari's first deputy to the French National Assembly. In 1949 he founded the Movement for the Social Evolution of Black Africa (MESAN), which became the vehicle of Central African nationalism. Boganda's ambition reached beyond his own territory: he envisioned a 'United States of Latin Africa' uniting the French Equatorial colonies and beyond.

On 1 December 1958 Boganda proclaimed the autonomous Central African Republic within the French Community, but he was killed in a plane crash on 29 March 1959, before independence. His relative David Dacko succeeded him, and on 13 August 1960 the Central African Republic became fully independent, with Dacko as its first president and Bangui as its capital.

events.title: Kongo-Wara rebellion
events.summary: The Gbaya prophet Karinou led a mass revolt against forced labour and railway recruitment; the French killed him in December 1928 and crushed the uprising by 1931, with thousands dead.

events.title: Boganda founds MESAN
events.summary: Barthélemy Boganda, the territory's first deputy to the French National Assembly, founded the Movement for the Social Evolution of Black Africa, the engine of Central African nationalism.

events.title: Death of Boganda
events.summary: Boganda, who had proclaimed the autonomous republic in December 1958 and championed a 'United States of Latin Africa', died in a plane crash months before independence; David Dacko succeeded him.

events.title: Independence from France
events.summary: The Central African Republic became fully independent, with David Dacko as its first president and Bangui as its capital.

pullquote.text: Boganda dreamed of a United States of Latin Africa; his death left the new republic to lesser men.
pullquote.attribution: On the legacy of Barthélemy Boganda

eras.title: Bokassa and the Age of Coups
eras.standfirst: Jean-Bédel Bokassa crowned himself emperor and was toppled by France, opening four decades of coups and instability.
body: On 1 January 1966 the army commander Jean-Bédel Bokassa overthrew his cousin David Dacko in the Saint-Sylvestre coup. He declared himself president for life in 1972 and, in December 1976, transformed the republic into the Central African Empire, crowning himself Emperor Bokassa I on 4 December 1977 in a coronation modelled on Napoleon's and estimated to have cost around US$20 million — roughly a third of the state's annual budget — while the country remained desperately poor.

His rule grew steadily more violent. In April 1979 protests against compulsory school uniforms produced by a company tied to his family ended in the killing of scores of schoolchildren and students detained in Bangui, an atrocity in which Bokassa was widely implicated. Having lost French backing, he was overthrown on 20–21 September 1979 by a French military operation (Barracuda) that restored Dacko; Bokassa fled into exile and was later tried and convicted in absentia and then in person.

Stability did not follow. General André Kolingba seized power from Dacko in 1981; multiparty elections in 1993 brought Ange-Félix Patassé to office; and in 2003 the army chief François Bozizé overthrew Patassé after a long insurgency. Each transition was shadowed by mutinies, foreign intervention and a state too weak to hold its territory.

events.title: Bokassa seizes power
events.summary: Army commander Jean-Bédel Bokassa overthrew President David Dacko in the Saint-Sylvestre coup, beginning more than a decade of personal rule.

events.title: Coronation of Emperor Bokassa I
events.summary: Having declared the Central African Empire in 1976, Bokassa crowned himself emperor in a lavish ceremony costing about US$20 million while the country remained among the world's poorest.

events.title: Killing of schoolchildren
events.summary: Protests against compulsory school uniforms ended in the massacre of scores of detained children and students in Bangui, an atrocity widely linked to Bokassa that hastened the end of his rule.

events.title: France overthrows Bokassa
events.summary: A French military operation (Barracuda) deposed Bokassa and restored David Dacko; Bokassa fled into exile and was later tried and convicted.

events.title: Bozizé seizes power
events.summary: After coups by André Kolingba (1981) and an elected interlude under Ange-Félix Patassé, army chief François Bozizé overthrew Patassé, continuing the cycle of armed takeovers.

eras.title: Séléka, Anti-balaka and a Fragile Peace
eras.standfirst: The 2013 Séléka coup triggered a sectarian civil war, leaving the country reliant on MINUSCA and foreign forces for survival.
body: Late in 2012 a mainly Muslim rebel alliance, Séléka, took up arms in the north, accusing President Bozizé of breaking earlier peace deals. On 24 March 2013 Séléka captured Bangui and Bozizé fled; its leader Michel Djotodia became the country's first Muslim head of state. Séléka abuses against civilians provoked the rise of the largely Christian and animist anti-balaka militias, and through late 2013 the country descended into reciprocal sectarian massacres, with attacks in Bangui in December 2013 killing more than a thousand people.

Under international pressure Djotodia resigned in January 2014 and Catherine Samba-Panza was chosen interim president. France deployed Operation Sangaris and the African Union a stabilisation force, and in April 2014 the UN Security Council authorised the peacekeeping mission MINUSCA, which took over in September 2014. Elections in early 2016 brought Faustin-Archange Touadéra to the presidency, but armed groups still controlled much of the interior.

A peace agreement signed in Khartoum in February 2019 brought only partial calm; a rebel coalition (the CPC) launched a major offensive around the December 2020 elections. From 2018 the government increasingly relied on Russian paramilitaries of the Wagner Group, alongside MINUSCA, to push back the rebels and hold the capital. The result is a fragile, externally propped peace over a state that has never fully recovered from the slave trade, colonial extraction and decades of coups.

events.title: Séléka captures Bangui
events.summary: The Séléka rebel coalition seized the capital and ousted President Bozizé; its leader Michel Djotodia declared himself president, the country's first Muslim head of state.

events.title: Sectarian civil war erupts
events.summary: Reprisals between Séléka and the anti-balaka militias spiralled into sectarian massacres; attacks in Bangui in early December 2013 killed more than a thousand civilians.

events.title: UN establishes MINUSCA
events.summary: After French and African Union deployments, the UN Security Council authorised the MINUSCA peacekeeping mission in April 2014; it assumed responsibility in September, alongside interim president Catherine Samba-Panza.

events.title: Khartoum peace agreement
events.summary: President Touadéra's government and fourteen armed groups signed a peace accord in Khartoum, but implementation was partial and fighting soon resumed.

events.title: CPC offensive and Wagner
events.summary: A rebel coalition (the CPC) attacked around the December 2020 elections; the government, increasingly backed by Russian Wagner Group mercenaries deployed from 2018, pushed it back with MINUSCA's help.

pullquote.text: A fragile peace held by UN blue helmets and Russian mercenaries over a state never fully healed of its history.
pullquote.attribution: On the Central African Republic since 2013

figures.name: Barthélemy Boganda
figures.role: Nationalist founder and first head of the autonomous republic
figures.blurb: A Roman Catholic priest who became Ubangi-Shari's first deputy to the French National Assembly and founded MESAN in 1949. He proclaimed the autonomous Central African Republic in December 1958 and dreamed of a 'United States of Latin Africa', but died in a plane crash in March 1959, months before independence.

figures.name: Jean-Bédel Bokassa
figures.blurb: Seized power in a 1966 coup, declared the Central African Empire and crowned himself Emperor Bokassa I in 1977. His rule, marked by extravagance and atrocities including the 1979 killing of schoolchildren, ended when France overthrew him; he was later tried and convicted.

figures.name: Faustin-Archange Touadéra
figures.blurb: A former prime minister and mathematics professor elected president in 2016 and re-elected in 2020. He has leaned heavily on the UN mission MINUSCA and on Russian Wagner Group paramilitaries to hold off rebel coalitions and assert control over the country.

sources.label: History of the Central African Republic
Company of the Upper Ubangi Sultanates
The Economic History of the Central African Republic
Movement for the Social Evolution of Black Africa (MESAN)
Remembering the national hero of the Central African Republic who died in a plane crash before independence
MINUSCA — United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in the Central African Republic
